title: Mango butter cold-process soap
subtitle: A firm, skin-conditioning bar built on mango butter and olive oil
excerpt: Mango butter has a higher stearic acid content than shea and melts just above body temperature, producing a harder, longer-lasting bar with a light, creamy skin feel. Combined with olive oil for conditioning and coconut oil for lather, the finished bar suits all skin types and sets to a smooth ivory.
sourceNotes: Mango butter fatty acid profile from USDA AAS database. SAP values from SoapCalc. Formulation ratios from Soap Queen (soapqueen.com, May 2026).

recipe.yieldDescription: 1 kg soap batter (~7 x 130 g bars after 4-week cure)
recipe.batchNotes: Scale proportionally. Recalculate lye through a soap calculator before making.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Cure for a minimum of 4 weeks. Mango butter bars are slow to cure to full hardness.
recipe.temperatureNote: Combine lye solution and oils when both are between 38 and 42 degrees Celsius.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: 2 years from cure date in a cool, dry place. Mango butter is a stable fat with a long shelf life and does not significantly shorten the bar's life. Discard if the bar smells rancid or shows dreaded orange spots.

glossaryTerms.definition: The percentage of oils left unsaponified in the finished bar. At 5%, it adds conditioning without leaving the bar greasy.
The reaction between sodium hydroxide and the fats in the oil blend that produces soap and glycerol. Each fat has a different SAP value that determines the amount of lye required.

The point where the blended oils and lye solution have emulsified enough to leave a visible trail on the surface when drizzled. Mango butter batters can take longer to reach trace than shea butter batches because of the higher stearic acid content.

content.text: Mango butter is pressed from the kernel inside the mango seed and has a fatty acid profile high in stearic and oleic acids. In cold-process soap it hardens the bar, slows the cure to trace, and produces a smooth surface after unmoulding. It is a direct substitute for cocoa butter and can be used interchangeably in most soap formulas. This formula runs a 5%
of mango butter's stearic acid produces a firm soap quickly; the bar can often be unmoulded after just 24 hours.
This recipe uses sodium hydroxide, a caustic alkali. Follow the
guidance before starting. Wear protective goggles and gloves throughout. Add lye to water in a well-ventilated space.

items.prepNote: 30% of oil weight; melt if solid
Run through a lye calculator to confirm

content.text: technique. Melt the mango butter and coconut oil together in a double boiler or a microwave in short bursts. Add the olive and castor oils. Mix the lye solution in the HDPE pitcher and allow both to cool to 38 to 42 degrees Celsius.
Pour the lye solution into the oils and blend to

. Mango butter batches can be slow to trace because of the high stearic acid content; keep stick-blending in short bursts. At light trace, pour into the mould and leave undisturbed for 24 to 48 hours. Unmould, cut into bars, and cure on a rack for a minimum of 4 weeks.

Cured bars keep for 2 years in a cool, dry place on an open rack. Mango butter is a stable fat and does not significantly increase the risk of rancidity. Discard any bar that smells sharp or shows orange spotting.
